_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Dive (SCUBA) Injury/Accidents. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Dive (SCUBA) Injury/Accidents

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Dive (SCUBA) Injury/Accidents
points: Rapid assessment and management of life-threatening injuries
Rescue from the water-based environment

Transport patients suffering from self-contained underwater breathing apparatus (SCUBA) diving injury/illness for hospital evaluation and consideration of repressurization/hyperbaric oxygen therapy (HBOT)

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients with history of recent (within 48 hours) SCUBA diving activity who are exhibiting potential signs and/or symptoms of dive related illness/injury, regardless of dive table compliance. NOTE: SCUBA-related complications may occur anywhere, particularly when divers travel by air within 24-hours of diving
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Patients without history of recent (within 48 hours) SCUBA diving exposure

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: If the patient is still in the water, seek safest and most rapid means of removal safe (within your scope of training) while minimizing risk of further injury
Seek assistance early for special rescue/extrication and transportation needs
Check for multiple patients (e.g., group dive table calculation error(s) or contaminated dive gases)

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Rescue efforts should be coordinated between all responding agencies to ensure that the patient is rapidly accessed and safely removed from the water if diver unable to do so themselves

If air medical transport is necessary, the patient should be transported with the cabin pressurized to lowest possible altitude. If an unpressurized aircraft is used (i.e., most helicopter emergency medical services (HEMS)), patient should be flown at the lowest safe altitude possible
Decompression illness may have a variety of presentations depending on system affected (e.g., skin, joint(s), pulmonary, neurologic)
SCUBA accidents/incidents can result in a variety of issues, including barotrauma, air embolism and decompression illness
Decompression illness may have a variety of presentations depending on system affected (e.g., skin, joint(s), pulmonary, neurologic), and can occur even when a diver does not exceed dive table limits
Do not attempt to disassemble, turn off, or modify any of the dive equipment. The dive computer may provide a clue about the patient's exposure to depth
Vital signs findings
Neurologic status assessment findings

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Water temperature, if available
' Dive history o Number of dives in recent history (days)
o "Bottom time" in dives
o Dive profiles
o Maximum depth
o Rate of ascent
o Safety stops utilized if any
o Dive gas (i.e., air vs. mixed gases such as Nitrox, Heliox or Trimix)

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
